region_of_origin: Java, Indonesia (Lamongan, Solo, Surabaya variants); widely adopted across the Malay Peninsula and Singapore
food_subgroup: Clear turmeric chicken noodle soup — shredded chicken, rice vermicelli, hard-boiled egg, bean sprouts, fried shallots in turmeric-lemongrass broth; 1 bowl ~500g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Soto Ayam. Closest parent would be a generic chicken soup or noodle soup concept. Null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken soup with noodles — closest parent match for Indonesian clear turmeric chicken soup (soto ayam); foodex2_match_type is closest_parent as no exact Soto Ayam code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GCB (chicken soup with noodles) used as closest defensible parent. No exact Soto Ayam entry in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Soto Ayam. Not a clinical entity requiring SNOMED mapping.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-stall serving of Soto Ayam is approximately 500g, comprising roughly 80g shredded chicken, 60g rice vermicelli, 280g clear turmeric-lemongrass broth, half a hard-boiled egg (~25g), and garnishes of fried shallots, bean sprouts, and Chinese celery (~55g).

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (2020) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.organization: FAO / INFOODS / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Soto Ayam nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 composite dish data for clear chicken turmeric soup, cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for similar Malay chicken soup preparations. Values represent the clear-broth (soto bening) variant as commonly served at Singapore Malay hawker stalls. Per-100g values are weighted averages across the full bowl composition (chicken, vermicelli, broth, egg, garnish).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli (GI ~58) used as primary carbohydrate anchor for Soto Ayam
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Soto Ayam as a composite dish. GI of 58 is anchored to rice vermicelli (GI ~58, medium), the primary carbohydrate component, per University of Sydney GI Database. The overall dish GI is moderated downward by chicken protein (~22g per bowl) and dietary fibre from bean sprouts, but the rice vermicelli GI of 58 is used as the conservative reference. GL calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 40g) / 100 = 23.2; input record reports 20.9 using a slightly lower effective carbohydrate estimate accounting for fibre; 20.9 retained from verified source cross-check.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~850mg per 500g bowl (~170mg/100g), representing approximately 37–42% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume Soto Ayam in moderation and consider requesting less broth or reduced seasoning, as the clear broth contributes the majority of sodium; at ~850mg per bowl it is lower than laksa (~1,200–1,500mg) or bak kut teh (~1,400mg) but still constitutes a significant single-meal sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 58 (rice vermicelli) with glycemic load ~20.9 per 500g bowl; glucose response attenuated by ~22g protein from shredded chicken and dietary fibre from bean sprouts
type2_diabetes.guidance: Soto Ayam is a reasonable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its moderate GI (58) and high protein content (~22g per bowl), which attenuates postprandial glucose rise; portion control remains important given the GL of ~20.9 per bowl, and patients should avoid adding sweetened condiments such as sambal kicap.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~3g per 500g bowl, 0.6g/100g) and low total fat (~10g per bowl) in the clear-broth version; cholesterol ~125mg per bowl from egg and chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The clear-broth Soto Ayam is a heart-friendly noodle soup option with low saturated fat and no trans fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the sodium content (~850mg per bowl) and opt for less broth to further reduce cardiovascular risk, while benefiting from the lean chicken protein and anti-inflammatory spice base of turmeric and lemongrass.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice base (turmeric, lemongrass, galangal) at culinary doses; low fat, moderate protein profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soto Ayam's turmeric-lemongrass broth provides modest anti-inflammatory benefit at culinary doses; the low-fat, high-protein profile is generally favourable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis compared to fatty meat-based dishes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soto Ayam is a clear yellow turmeric-lemongrass chicken soup originating from Java, Indonesia, and widely popular across Malaysia and Singapore. The dish is characterised by its golden broth coloured and flavoured with turmeric, lemongrass, galangal, ginger, and garlic, simmered with chicken until fragrant. It is served with shredded poached chicken, rice vermicelli (bee hoon), half a hard-boiled egg, fresh bean sprouts, and a garnish of crispy fried shallots and Chinese celery. In Singapore, the clear-broth version (soto bening) is the most common form found at Malay hawker stalls, distinct from the richer coconut milk-enriched Javanese variants such as Soto Betawi. Per standard 500g bowl: approximately 385 kcal, 22g protein, 10g fat (3g saturated), 40g carbohydrate, 2g dietary fibre, and ~850mg sodium. Glycemic index ~58 (medium, anchored to rice vermicelli), glycemic load ~20.9 per bowl. The high protein content from chicken significantly moderates the postprandial glucose response. Sodium is moderate relative to other hawker noodle soups (lower than laksa or bak kut teh). Suitable for halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. Nutritional data sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 and HPB Singapore Foodo database. Confidence: high.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 food composition table for Southeast Asian dishes, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database for Malay chicken soup preparations. GI anchored to rice vermicelli (GI 58) from University of Sydney GI Database. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) carry slightly lower confidence (0.9) as they were derived from enriched JSON estimates rather than direct source measurement, but all values are within plausible physiological ranges for this dish type.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Soto Ayam as a composite dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish (including broth) is not separately reported in the cited sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation; confirmed per FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 source). Copper (0.06mg/100g), manganese (0.1mg/100g), and vitamin K (5.0mcg/100g) values carry confidence 0.9 as they were derived from enriched JSON estimation rather than direct source measurement. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate anchored to rice vermicelli as the primary carbohydrate source; no direct GI measurement for Soto Ayam as a composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL of 20.9 per 500g bowl is calculated from the verified source cross-check. FoodEx2 code A0GCB used as closest parent (chicken soup with noodles); no exact Soto Ayam code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026. FoodOn URI is null — no specific FoodOn entry for Soto Ayam exists. Chinese names 沙爹鸡汤 / 沙爹雞湯 represent the colloquial Chinese reference to this dish in Singapore hawker contexts; 马来鸡汤 / 馬來雞湯 are also in common use. Hindi name सोटो अयाम is a transliteration used in multilingual Singapore contexts.
